terms.definition: A spoken or written description of an event, used to find out about the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We read an account of what school was like in Victorian times.
terms.common_errors: Confused with the banking meaning

terms.definition: Something important that a person or group has managed to do successfully.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Building the Great Wall was a remarkable achievement.

terms.definition: Later in time; following an event in chronological order.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: After the Great Fire, London was rebuilt with wider streets.

terms.definition: People in your family who lived a long time ago, further back than grandparents.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Our ancestors lived very differently from the way we live today.
terms.common_errors: Confused with living relatives

terms.definition: Belonging to a time very long ago, typically thousands of years in the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The ancient Egyptians built pyramids as tombs for their pharaohs.
terms.common_errors: Used loosely for anything old

terms.definition: A collection of historical documents or records kept so people can study the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The local archive has photographs of our town from 100 years ago.
terms.common_errors: Confused with a museum

terms.definition: An object made or used by people in the past that helps us learn about how they lived.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin arte factum, made by skill
terms.example_usage: The Roman artefact we found was a piece of broken pottery.
terms.common_errors: Confused with natural objects like fossils

terms.definition: Earlier in time than something else; preceding an event in chronological order.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Before the telephone, people sent letters to communicate.

terms.definition: A one-sided view that favours one opinion over another, shaped by the creators beliefs.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The diary might show bias because it only gives one persons point of view.
terms.common_errors: Assuming bias means a source is useless

terms.definition: A written account of someones life, telling what they did and why they mattered.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We read a biography of Florence Nightingale to learn about her work.
terms.common_errors: Confused with autobiography

terms.definition: An official count and survey of a population, recording details about how people live.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The census told us how many people lived in our village in 1901.
terms.common_errors: Thinking a census only counts people

terms.definition: A period of one hundred years, used to organise and talk about time in the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The 20th century began in 1901 and ended in 2000.
terms.common_errors: Confusing which century a year belongs to

terms.definition: When something becomes different over time, such as the way people live, work, or are governed.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: One big change was that children stopped working in factories.
terms.common_errors: Assuming all change is progress

terms.definition: To remember and honour a person or event from the past, often with a ceremony or monument.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We commemorate Remembrance Day to honour soldiers who died in wars.
terms.common_errors: Confused with celebrate, which implies joy

terms.definition: A group of people living in the same area or sharing common interests, traditions, or history.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The local community built a memorial for the people who lived here long ago.

terms.definition: When something stays the same over a period of time, even while other things change.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: There was continuity in farming methods for hundreds of years.
terms.common_errors: Thinking continuity means nothing happened

terms.definition: Something that a person or group did that helped bring about a change or achievement.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Mary Seacole made a great contribution to nursing during the Crimean War.

terms.definition: A period of ten years, used to describe and organise stretches of time.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Greek dekas, a group of ten
terms.example_usage: The 1960s was a decade of great change in Britain.
terms.common_errors: Confusing decade with century

terms.definition: To grow, change, or become more advanced over time.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Towns developed as more people moved to work in the new factories.
terms.common_errors: Assuming development always means improvement

terms.definition: The process of growing, changing, or becoming more advanced over a period of time.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The development of railways changed how people travelled across Britain.
terms.common_errors: Assuming development always means improvement

terms.definition: Not the same as something else; used in history to compare how things have changed.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Homes today are very different from homes in Tudor times.

terms.definition: To find or learn something for the first time, often through exploration or investigation.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Archaeologists discovered the remains of a Roman villa near our school.
terms.common_errors: Assuming discovery means something did not exist before

terms.definition: A written or printed record that provides information or evidence about the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The old document showed the names of everyone living in the village.
terms.common_errors: Thinking documents are always paper

terms.definition: Information from sources such as objects, documents, or pictures that helps us work out what happened.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The broken pottery is evidence that Romans once lived in this area.
terms.common_errors: Thinking evidence always proves something is true

terms.definition: Known about by many people, often because of important actions or achievements.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Neil Armstrong is famous for being the first person to walk on the Moon.
terms.common_errors: Equating famous with good

terms.definition: The time that has not yet happened; what will come after the present.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: In the future, people might look back on our time as the digital age.

terms.definition: The traditions, buildings, objects, and customs passed down from previous generations.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The castle is an important part of our local heritage.
terms.common_errors: Thinking heritage only means old buildings

terms.definition: A real person from the past who played an important role in events or changes.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Queen Victoria is a historical figure who ruled for over 60 years.
terms.common_errors: Confused with fictional characters

terms.definition: A place where important events happened in the past, often preserved for people to visit.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We visited the historical site where the Romans built a fort.

terms.definition: The qualities, beliefs, and history that make a person or group who they are.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Our schools identity is shaped by the communitys history.
terms.common_errors: Thinking identity is fixed rather than changing over time

terms.definition: The strong effect or influence that an event, person, or change has on what happens afterwards.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Great Fire of London had a huge impact on how the city was rebuilt.
terms.common_errors: Confused with the physical meaning of hitting something

terms.definition: To make something better or to become better over time.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Inventors worked to improve the way machines were powered.

terms.definition: To work out what something means by using clues from evidence rather than being told directly.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: From the old photograph, we can infer that the children wore uniforms.
terms.common_errors: Confused with guessing

terms.definition: To give someone the idea or motivation to do something, often by setting a powerful example.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin inspirare, to breathe into
terms.example_usage: Rosa Parks inspired others to stand up for their rights.

terms.definition: To explain the meaning of something, such as a source or event, based on the evidence.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Different people might interpret this painting in different ways.
terms.common_errors: Thinking there is only one correct interpretation

terms.definition: To create something that did not exist before, such as a machine, tool, or process.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Alexander Graham Bell invented the telephone in 1876.
terms.common_errors: Confused with discover

terms.definition: A well-known building, monument, or feature that is easily recognised and historically important.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Big Ben is a famous London landmark.

terms.definition: To guide or be in charge of a group of people, especially during important events.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Boudicca led the Iceni tribe in a revolt against the Romans.
terms.common_errors: Confused with the metal lead

terms.definition: Something left behind by a person, group, or event from the past that still affects us today.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Roman legacy includes roads and towns that we still use.
terms.common_errors: Thinking legacy only means positive things

terms.definition: Relating to the nearby area where you live; describing events or features of your own community.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We studied the local history of our town by visiting the old church.

terms.definition: A time in the distant past, much further back than living memory.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Long ago, people used candles because there was no electricity.
terms.common_errors: Used vaguely without specifying how long ago

terms.definition: Belonging to the present time or the recent past, as opposed to earlier historical periods.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Modern schools are very different from schools in the 1800s.
terms.common_errors: In history, modern can mean a specific period rather than right now

terms.definition: Relating to a whole country or nation, rather than just one local area.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Remembrance Day is a national event when the whole country remembers.

terms.definition: Recently made, discovered, or introduced; not existing before.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The printing press was a new way to share information.

terms.definition: To look at something carefully in order to notice details and gather information.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We observed the old photograph to find clues about how people dressed.
terms.common_errors: Confused with just looking casually

terms.definition: Having existed for a long time; belonging to an earlier period in the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The old map showed what our town looked like before the railway came.

terms.definition: Stories and memories about the past spoken aloud and passed on by word of mouth.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Grandma shared oral history about what life was like during the war.
terms.common_errors: Thinking oral history is less valid than written records

terms.definition: The arrangement of events or objects in a sequence, from first to last.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We put the pictures in order to show how transport changed over time.
terms.common_errors: Confused with a command or instruction

terms.definition: The time before now; everything that has already happened.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: In the past, people washed clothes by hand in a river.
terms.common_errors: Confused with passed (verb form)

terms.definition: A picture taken with a camera, used in history as evidence about events and people.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The old photograph showed children working in a Victorian factory.
terms.common_errors: Assuming photographs always show the full truth

terms.definition: A person who is among the first to explore, settle, or develop a new area or idea.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Elizabeth Garrett Anderson was a pioneer of women in medicine.

terms.definition: The current time; now, as opposed to the past or the future.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We compared how houses looked in the past with how they look in the present.
terms.common_errors: Confused with the meaning of a gift

terms.definition: Evidence created at the time of the event being studied, such as a letter or diary.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The soldiers letter is a primary source because he wrote it during the war.
terms.common_errors: Primary does not mean more important, it means from the time

terms.definition: Movement forward or improvement over time, often in knowledge, technology, or living conditions.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The invention of the wheel was an important step of progress.
terms.common_errors: Assuming all change counts as progress

terms.definition: A sentence used to find out information; in history, the starting point for investigating the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Our history question was: How did people travel before cars were invented?

terms.definition: Having happened a short time ago; near to the present.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The invention of the internet is a recent event in history.

terms.definition: A written or stored account of information or events, kept so people can look back.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The parish record listed all the births and deaths in the village.
terms.common_errors: Confused with a music or sports record

terms.definition: Trustworthy and likely to be accurate; a source that can be depended on.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We need to decide if this diary is reliable before using it as evidence.
terms.common_errors: Assuming official sources are always reliable

terms.definition: Identical or unchanged; used when comparing things that have not altered over time.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Some games children play today are the same as those played 100 years ago.

terms.definition: Evidence created after the event by someone who was not there, such as a textbook.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Our textbook is a secondary source because it was written long after the events.
terms.common_errors: Thinking secondary means less useful

terms.definition: A set of events or objects placed in a particular order, from first to last.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We put the events in sequence to see how the story unfolded.

terms.definition: A place where people come to live together, from small villages to large towns.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Romans built a settlement near the river for fresh water.
terms.common_errors: Confused with settling an agreement

terms.definition: Important enough to have an effect on what happens or to be worth remembering.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Moon landing was a significant event in world history.
terms.common_errors: Small events can be significant if they cause change

terms.definition: Anything that gives us information about the past, including objects, documents, and buildings.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The old map is a useful source for learning about our town in 1850.
terms.common_errors: Thinking all sources are written

terms.definition: A line showing events in the order they happened, with dates marked along it.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We made a timeline to show when each king and queen ruled.
terms.common_errors: Not spacing events proportionally to time gaps

terms.definition: A custom or belief that has been passed down through generations and is still practised.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin traditio, a handing over
terms.example_usage: Bonfire Night is a tradition that has continued for over 400 years.
terms.common_errors: Assuming traditions have always existed in their current form

terms.definition: To change something completely in form, appearance, or character.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The railways transformed the way people travelled across Britain.

terms.definition: A period of twelve months, used as a basic unit for measuring and dating events in history.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Great Fire of London happened in the year 1666.
